perturbation.original_text:
Either party may cancel this Agreement upon the occurrence of any of the following:
Material breach of any covenant, term, condition or other provisions of this Agreement, which breach is not remedied within ten (10) days after notice of such breach is received by the breaching party
perturbation.changed_text:
Either party may cancel this Agreement upon the occurrence of any of the following:
Material breach of any covenant, term, condition or other provisions of this Agreement, which breach is not remedied within two (2) days after notice of such breach is received by the breaching party
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach to two days may be unreasonably short and conflict with general contract law principles that require a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Reasonable Opportunity to Cure
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Nevada law and general contract principles, parties are afforded a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach of contract. A two-day cure period may be deemed unreasonable depending on the nature and complexity of the breach, making the termination clause potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NRS 104.2605 Waiver of buyer's objections by failure to particularize. :: Chapter 104 Uniform Commercial Code — Original Articles :: TITLE 8 COMMERCIAL INSTRUMENTS AND TRANSACTIONS :: 2010 Nevada Revised Statutes :: Nevada Revised Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Nevada Revised Statutes 2024 Nevada Rev. Stat. 2023 Nevada Rev. Stat. 2022 Nevada Rev. Stat. 2010 Nevada Rev. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2010 Nevada Code TITLE 8 COMMERCIAL INSTRUMENTS AND TRANSACTIONS Chapter 104 Uniform Commercial Code — Original Articles NRS 104.2605 Waiver of buyer's objections by failure to particularize. NRS 104.2605 Waiver of buyer's objections by failure to particularize. 1. The buyer's failure to state in connection with rejection a particular defect which is ascertainable by reasonable inspection precludes the buyer from relying on the unstated defect to justify rejection or to establish breach: (a) Where the seller could have cured it if stated seasonably; or (b) Between merchants when the seller has after rejection made a request in writing for a full and final written statement of all defects on which the buyer proposes to rely. 2. Payment against documents made without reservation of rights precludes recovery of the payment for defects apparent in the documents. (Added to NRS by 1965, 806; A 2005, 852) Disclaimer: These codes may not be the most recent version. Nevada may have more current or accurate information. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period for a material breach from ten days to two days. The Law Explanation states that 'parties are afforded a reasonable opportunity to cure a breach of contract' under Nevada law and general contract principles. The Perturbation Explanation states that 'reducing the cure period for a material breach to two days may be unreasonably short'. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 does not directly address cure periods, it implies that a reasonable opportunity to cure a defect is expected by stating '(a) Where the seller could have cured it if stated seasonably'. The reduction to two days contradicts the principle of providing a reasonable opportunity to cure, making the contractual term potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: MA shall notify Company in writing of any errors found by it in the Technology within thirty (30) days of such discovery.
perturbation.changed_text: MA shall notify Company in writing of any errors found by it in the Technology within five (5) days of such discovery.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period for errors to five days may be insufficient for a thorough assessment, potentially conflicting with implied warranties or reasonable business practices.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-607 requires a buyer to notify the seller of any breach within a reasonable time after discovery. Five days may not always be considered a reasonable time, particularly for complex software or technical errors, potentially limiting the buyer's remedies for breach of warranty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-607. Effect of Acceptance; Notice of Breach; Burden of Establishing Breach After Acceptance; Notice of Claim or Litigation to Person Answerable Over. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-607. Effect of Acceptance; Notice of Breach; Burden of Establishing Breach After Acceptance; Notice of Claim or Litigation to Person Answerable Over. (1) The buyer must pay at the contract rate for any goods accepted. (2) Acceptance of goods by the buyer precludes rejection of the goods accepted and if made with knowledge of a non-conformity cannot be revoked because of it unless the acceptance was on the reasonable assumption that the non-conformity would be seasonably cured but acceptance does not of itself impair any other remedy provided by this Article for non-conformity. (3)Where a tender has been accepted (a) the buyer must within a reasonable time after he discovers or should have discovered any breach notify the seller of breach or be barred from any remedy; and (b) if the claim is one for infringement or the like (subsection (3) of Section 2-312 ) and the buyer is sued as a result of such a breach he must so notify the seller within a reasonable time after he receives notice of the litigation or be barred from any remedy over for liability established by the litigation. (4) The burden is on the buyer to establish any breach with respect to the goods accepted. (5) Where the buyer is sued for breach of a warranty or other obligation for which his seller is answerable over (a) he may give his seller written notice of the litigation. If the notice states that the seller may come in and defend and that if the seller does not do so he will be bound in any action against him by his buyer by any determination of fact common to the two litigations, then unless the seller after seasonable receipt of the notice does come in and defend he is so bound. (b) if the claim is one for infringement or the like
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification period for errors from thirty (30) days to five (5) days. The law snippet from UCC § 2-607 states that the buyer must notify the seller of breach within a 'reasonable time' after discovery. The Law Explanation argues that five days may not always be considered a 'reasonable time,' especially for complex errors, potentially limiting the buyer's remedies. Therefore, reducing the notification period to five days creates a contradiction because it may not align with the 'reasonable time' requirement stipulated in the UCC, potentially making the contractual term unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of a price and/or fee change for Technology, Company shall notify MA in writing ninety (90) days prior to the effective date of any such change.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of a price and/or fee change for Technology, Company shall notify MA in writing fifteen (15) days prior to the effective date of any such change.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the price change notification period to fifteen days may be insufficient for MA to adjust its business strategies, potentially violating principles of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-305(2) requires good faith in setting prices, and a sudden price change with only fifteen days' notice might be deemed commercially unreasonable and a violation of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, depending on the industry and circumstances. Nevada adopted this law.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a 90-day notice for price changes, while the modified text reduces this to 15 days. This inconsistency, as explained in the 'Perturbation Explanation' and 'Law Explanation', could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing under UCC § 2-305(2), especially if the reduced notice period is deemed commercially unreasonable. While the provided snippets are unhelpful, the core issue stems from a legally significant time-sensitive obligation being altered to potentially violate established commercial norms, which aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction'.
